ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Pepper Chicken Masala; closest parent 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03302757) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Pepper Chicken Masala; A0CKP (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 'Chicken curry' is the closest available term; no specific Pepper Chicken Masala code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Pepper Chicken Masala is approximately 250 g, comprising bone-in or boneless chicken pieces in a thick spiced gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, prepared — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports values for chicken curry (cooked, with spices and oil). Pepper Chicken Masala is a dry-style variant with higher black pepper content; values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN chicken curry entry with minor adjustment for the drier gravy profile. Core macros are consistent with USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, stewed' cross-referenced for protein and fat plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pepper Chicken Masala. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous mixed chicken curry dishes with low-GI spice-dominant gravies reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate (4.5 g/100 g), yielding a low GI consistent with high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes. GL per 250 g serving = (35 × 4.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 3.9.

hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (1050 mg per 250 g serving), Pepper Chicken Masala contributes a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 35 and only 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Pepper Chicken Masala has a low glycemic load and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pepper Chicken Masala contains approximately 2.4 g saturated fat and 420 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size, choose lean chicken cuts, and request preparation with minimal added oil to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pepper Chicken Masala is a popular South Indian and Indian restaurant dish consisting of chicken pieces — typically bone-in or boneless — cooked in a bold, aromatic masala sauce dominated by freshly ground or coarsely cracked black pepper. The dish is prepared by marinating chicken in a blend of black pepper, turmeric, ginger, and garlic, then sautéing with onions, tomatoes, curry leaves, and whole spices such as cumin and coriander in oil. The result is a semi-dry to moderately gravied dish with an intensely peppery, savoury, and mildly spicy flavour profile and a tender, well-seasoned texture. Pepper Chicken Masala is a staple of South Indian cuisine, particularly associated with Tamil Nadu and Kerala, and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader South and Southeast Asian diaspora. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-glycaemic dietary patterns. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically prepared halal. Key clinical considerations include its moderate sodium content from added salt and spice blends, and its saturated fat contribution from cooking oil and chicken skin. The black pepper component provides piperine, which has been studied for its bioavailability-enhancing properties.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry data cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken entries. No exact match for Pepper Chicken Masala exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate based on comparable high-protein, low-carbohydrate chicken curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than low because the macronutrient profile is well-supported by the combination of ICMR-NIN and USDA sources for the core ingredients.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category and is not reliably present in cooked chicken curry preparations at measurable levels in the cited databases. All other -1 micronutrient fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) have been populated with estimated values from ICMR-NIN and USDA cross-reference where available; only vitamin_d_mcg remains at -1 as it is not reported in the source database. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Pepper Chicken Masala was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Sodium value reflects typical restaurant preparation; home-cooked versions may have lower sodium. The dish is served at Anjana Kitchen, Six Battery Road, Singapore, at SGD 22.50 per serving (source: field survey data, preserved from input record for context only — not used as a nutritional source).
